confidence.disclosure: Sultan Mehmed II (Mehmed Fatih), the conqueror of Constantinople, is exceptionally well documented in Greek (Doukas, Kritoboulos, Sphrantzes), Ottoman (Tursun Beg, Ashikpashazade), and western European sources. The major dates - his birth in Edirne in 1432, his first reign of 1444-1446 and second reign from 1451, the conquest of Constantinople on 29 May 1453, his subsequent campaigns in the Balkans, Anatolia, and the Aegean, and his death in 1481 - are all firm by year and most by day. The Mehmed of this journey is the most central figure of 15th-century Mediterranean history.

sources: Kritoboulos of Imbros, History of Mehmed the Conqueror
Franz Babinger, Mehmed the Conqueror and His Time (1978)

journey.event: Born on 30 March 1432 in Edirne, then the Ottoman capital, the third son of Sultan Murad II. His mother Hüma Hatun is most likely a slave concubine of obscure origin (Greek or Slavic). The 30 March birthdate is given by Tursun Beg.

Sent to Manisa in western Anatolia as governor (sancak beg), the traditional training post for Ottoman princes. After the deaths of his two elder brothers (the elder Ahmed in 1437, Ala al-Din in February 1443), Mehmed is the only surviving son and the heir.

In August 1444, after his father Murad II abdicates following the Peace of Edirne, the twelve-year-old Mehmed is enthroned as sultan in Edirne. His first reign is brief and difficult: a Christian crusade led by John Hunyadi crosses the Danube, and Mehmed has to recall his father from retirement to lead the army.

On 10 November 1444 his father Murad wins the Battle of Varna against the crusade. Cardinal Cesarini and King Wladyslaw III of Poland are killed. The young Mehmed is in Edirne during the battle but the victory secures the Ottoman position. Murad reluctantly resumes the throne in 1446 and Mehmed returns to Manisa.

On 3 February 1451 Murad II dies in Edirne. Mehmed, eighteen years old, rides post-haste from Manisa to Edirne and is enthroned again, this time permanently, on 18 February 1451. He immediately has his infant half-brother Ahmed strangled in the bath, and codifies the practice of fratricide on accession in his later law code.

In the spring of 1452 begins building the great fortress of Rumeli Hisari on the European shore of the Bosphorus, opposite the older Anadolu Hisari on the Asian side. The fortress is built in just four months and gives Mehmed control of the strait. The Byzantines understand that the conquest of Constantinople is now imminent.

On 6 April 1453 the Ottoman army arrives before the walls of Constantinople. Mehmed has assembled an army of 80,000-150,000 men with the largest siege artillery yet seen in warfare, including the great bombard cast for him by the Hungarian engineer Orban, capable of firing a stone ball weighing 600 kg. The siege lasts 53 days.

On the night of 21-22 April 1453, in one of the most famous logistical feats of the medieval world, Mehmed has about 70 ships dragged overland on greased logs from the Bosphorus over the hill of Galata and into the Golden Horn, bypassing the chain that the Byzantines had stretched across the harbour mouth. The Byzantines, attacked from a direction they had thought safe, are forced to defend a much longer line of walls.

On 29 May 1453 (20 Jumada I 857 AH), in the early hours, the final Ottoman assault breaks through the walls near the gate of St Romanus. The last Roman emperor Constantine XI Dragases is killed in the fighting. Mehmed enters the city later in the day, rides to Hagia Sophia, dismounts in the courtyard and pours a handful of dust on his turban as a gesture of humility, then enters the great church and orders it converted into a mosque. The Roman Empire, founded 1480 years earlier, comes to an end.

In July 1456 lays siege to Belgrade, then a Hungarian fortress on the Danube. The defence is led by John Hunyadi and the Italian friar John of Capistrano. The Ottomans are repulsed in heavy fighting on 22 July; Mehmed himself is wounded by an arrow in the thigh. It is one of his very few defeats. (Hunyadi dies of plague three weeks later.)

In August 1461 takes Trebizond, the last Greek imperial state, ruled by the Komnenoi on the Black Sea coast. The emperor David Komnenos and his family are deported to Constantinople and later executed. With this conquest the last fragment of the Roman Empire ceases to exist.

In 1463 conquers the Kingdom of Bosnia, executing the last Bosnian king Stjepan Tomasevic. Bosnia is incorporated as an Ottoman province. The conquest brings about the gradual conversion of much of the local population to Islam.

On 11 August 1473 wins the decisive Battle of Otlukbeli in eastern Anatolia against the Aq Qoyunlu sultan Uzun Hasan, the last great Turkmen rival in Anatolia and a man who had been intriguing with Venice against the Ottomans. The Ottoman victory establishes Mehmed as the unchallenged power in the eastern Islamic world after the Mamluks.

In August 1480 Mehmed's fleet under Gedik Ahmed Pasha lands at Otranto on the heel of Italy and seizes the city. Mehmed plans to use it as a bridgehead for the conquest of Italy and Rome itself. At the same time another Ottoman force unsuccessfully besieges Rhodes, defended by the Knights Hospitaller. The threat of an Ottoman landing in Italy throws western Europe into panic.

Dies on 3 May 1481 (4 Rabi I 886 AH) at his camp at Tekfur Cayiri (Hunkar Cayiri) east of Constantinople, while preparing a new expedition - whose target (Egypt or Italy) remains unknown. He is 49 years old; the cause of death is most probably gout, though poisoning by his own physician at the order of his son Bayezid has been suspected by some historians. His body is taken to Constantinople and buried in the Fatih (Conqueror) mosque complex he built on the fourth hill, where his tomb still stands. The Mediterranean world breathes a collective sigh of relief; in Rome, church bells ring in celebration.
